storm.summary_layperson: A line of thunderstorm cells is approaching from the northwest with arrivals possible as early as 6 PM — the Long Island Sound's cool water is likely to weaken them on the way in, so the cove may get gusty winds while areas two miles inland take more thunder and heavier rain.
storm.local_effect_blurb: The Sound's 71°F water against 84°F inland air creates a 13°F gap that builds a moderate stable layer over the water, typically choking low-level convection before it reaches the cove.
storm.detail_html:
Radar shows three storm cells 34–36 mi to the WNW through NNW, tracked moving toward the cove at 23 kt ENE (measured centroid motion across two KOKX scans). The leading pair — bearing WNW at 44 dBZ and NW at 43 dBZ — are closing at 13–15 kt with ETAs of 6:28 PM and 6:42 PM. A slower third cell to the NNW at 36 dBZ has a late-night ETA around 10:36 PM.

NWS forecasters cite SBCAPE around 2,000 J/kg with DCAPE exceeding 1,000 J/kg and a "severe wind threat where storms reach their equilibrium level around 40,000 ft." HRRR shows only 660 J/kg — the AFD figure reflects actual soundings and is more representative here. Bulk shear of 42.6 kt (WSW) combined with 2,000 J/kg CAPE produces organized convection with genuine gusty wind potential.

The protective mechanism is active. Long Island Sound sits at 71°F against 84°F inland air — a 13°F air–water gap — and the storm approach crosses the Sound nearly perpendicularly from the WNW–NW, which maximizes the marine layer's effect. Measured storm motion of 23 kt (moderate speed, not a fast squall line) also favors deflection. At sub-3,000 J/kg CAPE, the stable marine boundary layer can choke low-level convection as cells cross the water. Most likely outcome: the cove sees gusty winds and possibly some rain; areas two to three miles inland take more thunder and heavier precipitation.

The first cell's early edge opens around 5:57 PM. The late edge stretches to 7:24 PM because the marine layer may slow or weaken the cells over the Sound. Watch live radar for the WNW cell's reflectivity trend as it approaches the CT shore — if it drops below 35 dBZ over the water, the Sound effect is working as expected and storm risk here drops significantly.

What would change the call upward: an upstream APRS station near Rye or Port Chester spiking gusts above 25 kt before the listed ETA means the gust front is outrunning radar reflectivity — lean to an earlier, stronger arrival. Conversely, cells fading below 35 dBZ over the Sound is a clean signal the threat is weakening. No NWS warnings or watches are currently in effect.

arrival.blurb: Radar-tracked cell 35 mi WNW closing at 15 kt, with a near-identical 43 dBZ cell immediately behind at 6:42 PM — the marine layer may slow or weaken both as they cross the Sound, biasing arrival toward the late edge.

wind.regime_label: Conflicting winds — offshore gradient fighting the sea breeze

hourly.shift_note: gradient W, holding ahead of approaching cells
W, storm cells closing — gust front possible
W–WNW, storm gust front arrival window; temporary spike likely

wind.tactics_blurb: With the offshore gradient winning the W–WNW battle at western Long Island Sound and storm cells arriving during the early part of the window, racing before 8 PM is not advisable — if conditions clear, the post-storm NW at 5–7 kt favors a right-side bias with the best pressure in the center of the course.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
The air–water temperature difference of 13.1°F (air 84°F, Sound water 71°F) provides strong thermal energy for a sea breeze — and one is indeed running across central and eastern Long Island Sound, with KBDR, KHVN, KGON, and Block Island all showing SSW at 7–16 kt. That sea breeze has not penetrated western LIS. RYC reads WNW 288° at 12.6 kt gusting 19.3 kt, and nearby Old Greenwich and Port Chester APRS stations confirm WNW–NW flow. The offshore gradient of roughly 10 kt is beating the sea breeze at the western end of the Sound. This is a conflicting-winds day: two flows competing, with the gradient winning here while the sea breeze wins everything east of Bridgeport.

HRRR shows a steady clockwise veer through the window — W 271°→272°→279° at 7–9 kt for 17:00–19:00, then WNW 294° at 7 kt around 20:00, NW 306° at 6 kt at 21:00, and fading to roughly 6 kt NW by 22:00. Sunset at approximately 8:15 PM removes any remaining thermal component; thereafter the flow is pure gradient and continues to veer and weaken. Note that HRRR is under-reading the cove by about 4 kt relative to the current RYC observation (HRRR f01 shows 8.3 kt while RYC simultaneously reads 12.6 kt). The storm-approach environment or localized acceleration at the cove mouth may be inflating the reading — actual speeds through the window may run somewhat higher than the hourly table reflects.

Three storm cells are closing from WNW through NNW with the lead cell's early edge arriving as soon as 5:57 PM. Even under the marine-layer protection scenario — the more likely outcome given the 13°F air–water delta — a gust front will precede precipitation. Expect a sharp temporary wind spike to WNW–NW, possibly reaching 20–25 kt, around the 19:00 slot, followed by a lull as the storm passes. The hourly table shows background HRRR gradient flow; during storm passage actual gusts will exceed those figures.

With W–WNW flow crossing the N–S cove axis, wind enters from the western shore rather than running up the cove. The "holes near the CT shore" pattern applies — weaker, more irregular pressure close to the north shore, better flow in the center of the course and toward mid-Sound. Shift character is not the regular oscillations of a mature sea-breeze day; this is gradient-and-convective puffs separated by lulls. As the gradient steadily veers from W toward NW through the window, the right side of every upwind leg gains persistently — the veer is continuous, not a two-way oscillation, so commit right rather than playing it as a tactical shift.

The storm arrival window (5:57–7:44 PM) overlaps the early part of the evening series. Even with the marine layer limiting precipitation, racing in organized 40+ kt shear with 43–44 dBZ cells approaching is not recommended. If the cells weaken below 35 dBZ while crossing the Sound, post-storm conditions from roughly 20:00 onward will offer light NW at 5–7 kt — marginal but raceable, with a clean right-bias call. Monitor live radar: if the WNW cells hold intensity across the Sound, wait for passage before sending the fleet out.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 (WLIS) offline — UConn ERDDAP 404; over-water sea-breeze depth and wind signals unavailable.
KHPN anemometer down (outlier); inland Westchester wind not available.
KISP anemometer down (outlier); south-shore LI wind not available.
AFD SBCAPE (2,000 J/kg) significantly exceeds HRRR CAPE (660 J/kg); storm risk assessed using the AFD figure as more representative of the actual atmospheric state.
